summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a reference frame?

A reference frame is a coordinate system with respect to which position, velocity, and other quantities are measured. All motion is relative to a chosen reference frame.

What is an inertial frame?

An inertial frame is a reference frame that is not accelerating (moving at constant velocity or at rest). Newton's laws of motion hold directly in inertial frames without needing corrections.

Two cars approach each other, one at 50 km/h and the other at 60 km/h. What is their relative speed?

When two objects move toward each other (opposite directions), their relative speed is the sum: 50 + 60 = 110 km/h. Each car sees the other approaching at 110 km/h.

A person walks at 2 m/s on a train moving at 30 m/s in the same direction. What is the person's speed relative to the ground?

When moving in the same direction, velocities add: 30 + 2 = 32 m/s relative to the ground. The person's ground speed is the sum of the train speed and walking speed.

What is a 'non-inertial frame'?

A non-inertial frame is accelerating (speeding up, slowing down, or rotating). Newton's laws do not directly apply in non-inertial frames without adding fictitious forces like the centrifugal force.

What is the 'speed of light' and why is it important for relative motion?

The speed of light is approximately 3 x 10^8 m/s. At speeds approaching this value, Galilean velocity addition no longer works and must be replaced by Einstein's relativistic formulas.

A passenger on a train moving at 25 m/s throws a ball forward at 5 m/s relative to the train. What is the ball's speed relative to the ground?

The ball's ground velocity = train velocity + ball's velocity relative to train = 25 + 5 = 30 m/s forward. Both velocities are in the same direction, so they add.

A boat heads north at 4 m/s in still water. The river current flows east at 3 m/s. What is the boat's actual speed relative to the ground?

North and east are perpendicular, so use the Pythagorean theorem: speed = sqrt(4^2 + 3^2) = sqrt(16 + 9) = sqrt(25) = 5 m/s.

A plane flies north at 200 km/h relative to the air. A wind blows from west to east at 50 km/h. What is the plane's speed relative to the ground?

The northward and eastward velocities are perpendicular: speed = sqrt(200^2 + 50^2) = sqrt(40000 + 2500) = sqrt(42500) = approximately 206 km/h.

A student walks backward at 1 m/s on a moving walkway that travels forward at 2 m/s. What is the student's velocity relative to the ground?

Walking backward at 1 m/s on a walkway moving forward at 2 m/s: ground velocity = 2 + (-1) = 1 m/s forward. The walkway moves faster forward than the student walks backward.

A student on a skateboard moving at 3 m/s throws a ball backward at 3 m/s relative to themselves. What is the ball's velocity relative to the ground?

Ground velocity = skateboard velocity + ball's velocity relative to skateboard = 3 + (-3) = 0 m/s. The ball momentarily has zero horizontal velocity and drops straight down from the ground observer's perspective.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A helicopter hovers above a moving truck. A package is dropped from the helicopter. Where does the package land relative to the truck?

The hovering helicopter has zero horizontal velocity, so the dropped package also has zero horizontal velocity. The truck moves forward while the package falls straight down, so the package lands behind the truck.
